title: Monotonicity of Data Along Ricci Flow on Surfaces
abstract: Consider a closed surface M of genus greater than or equal to 2. For negatively curved metrics on M and their corresponding geodesic flow, we can study the topological entropy, the Liouville entropy, and the mean root curvature. In 2004, Manning showed that the topological entropy strictly decreases along the normalized Ricci flow if we start with a metric of variable negative curvature and asked whether monotonicity holds for the Liouville entropy. In this talk, we answer Manning's question and show that the Liouville entropy strictly increases along the flow. This talk is based on joint work with Butt, Humbert, and Mitsutani.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Uniformization and normalized Ricci flow on negatively curved surfaces
proof_stages.summary: For a closed oriented surface of genus at least two, Gauss-Bonnet and uniformization identify the constant negative curvature representative in each conformal class, while normalized Ricci flow preserves area and conformal class, preserves negativity of curvature, and converges to that representative.

proof_stages.label: Mean root curvature monotone
proof_stages.summary: The mean root curvature, defined as the area average of sqrt(-K), is introduced as curvature data and stated to increase strictly along normalized Ricci flow unless the initial metric already has constant curvature.

proof_stages.label: Geodesic-flow entropy data
proof_stages.summary: For negatively curved metrics, the geodesic flow on the unit tangent bundle is introduced together with measure entropy, the Liouville measure and its entropy, topological entropy as a supremum over invariant measures, and the entropy-maximizing measure.

proof_stages.label: Geometric formulas and entropy rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Manning's volume-entropy formula and the horosphere/Riccati formula for Liouville entropy give geometric control of the entropies, leading to the constant-curvature reference value and rigidity or strict inequalities among topological entropy, Liouville entropy, mean root curvature, and that reference value.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness of static entropy inequalities
proof_stages.summary: In response to a question about entropy gaps, the speaker explains that the known strict inequalities impose no additional uniform separation over all negative curvature metrics of fixed area, since admissible entropy pairs can be realized by suitable metric constructions.

proof_stages.label: Ricci-flow monotonicity theorem for entropy data
proof_stages.summary: Manning's theorem that topological entropy strictly decreases along normalized Ricci flow is recalled, and the new result is stated: Liouville entropy strictly increases, so the three data sets h_top, h_L, and mean root curvature converge monotonically to the constant-curvature value.

proof_stages.label: Variational reduction for Liouville entropy
proof_stages.summary: Using the fact that normalized Ricci flow is a conformal deformation and using differentiability of entropy, the proof reduces the Liouville-entropy monotonicity to a first-variation formula involving the conformal factor derivative and stable horosphere curvature, parallel to the known topological-entropy variation formula.

proof_stages.label: Positivity argument and analytic tools
proof_stages.summary: Substituting the Ricci-flow variation into the first-variation formula and using Riccati relations plus a Jensen/convexity-type inequality yields positivity of the derivative, while the derivation of the formula relies on boundary-at-infinity coordinates for the unit tangent bundle and microlocal analysis of half-orbit integrals.

examples.name: Pair-of-pants/Fenchel-Nielsen picture of hyperbolic surfaces
examples.note: A genus-two surface cut into pairs of pants, with boundary lengths and twists varied, illustrates the abundance of constant negative curvature metrics in a fixed topological type without driving the later proof.
examples.engagement_reason: She draws the genus-two surface, cuts it into pairs of pants, and reasons through choosing cuff lengths and twist gluings in the Fenchel–Nielsen picture to produce varied hyperbolic metrics.

passes.reason: a standard Fenchel–Nielsen pants decomposition gives ordinary hyperbolic surfaces and is used illustratively to show abundance of metrics, not to refute a claim
Standard Fenchel–Nielsen pants-decomposition picture illustrating the abundance of ordinary hyperbolic surface metrics, not used to disprove anything.
A standard Fenchel–Nielsen pants decomposition describes ordinary hyperbolic surfaces/moduli and is used illustratively, not to refute a claim.
A standard pair-of-pants/Fenchel–Nielsen decomposition is used illustratively to show the abundance and parametrization of ordinary hyperbolic surface metrics, not to refute a claim.
Used as a standard illustrative pants decomposition/Fenchel–Nielsen parametrization showing many ordinary hyperbolic metrics, not to refute a claim.
A pants decomposition/Fenchel–Nielsen coordinate description is a standard parametrization of ordinary hyperbolic surfaces, used to illustrate abundance rather than to refute a claim.

It is used as the standard Fenchel-Nielsen parametrization illustrating many hyperbolic metrics, not as an obstruction, and represents ordinary decomposed hyperbolic surfaces rather than an extremal, collapsed, highly symmetric, or sporadic object.

Used as the standard Fenchel-Nielsen parametrization/decomposition illustrating the abundance of hyperbolic surface metrics, not to refute a claim and not structurally special beyond being a routine building-block picture.

Used as a standard illustrative parametrization of ordinary hyperbolic surfaces by pants lengths and twists, not to refute a claim and not structurally sporadic, maximally symmetric, collapsed, or extremal.
Used as the standard Fenchel–Nielsen parametrization illustrating ordinary hyperbolic metrics on higher-genus surfaces, not to refute a claim or exhibit a special boundary case.
A standard pair-of-pants/Fenchel–Nielsen decomposition is used illustratively to parametrize many ordinary hyperbolic surface metrics, not to refute a claim.
Used as the standard Fenchel–Nielsen parametrization showing there are many hyperbolic metrics on a surface, with no sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximal-symmetry feature singled out.
